nativeRange.habitat: Lakes, streams, and shallow wetlands across the southeastern United States, extending into northeastern Mexico

decorPreferences: background or midground placement in groups

summary: A delicate North American stem plant with fine, needle-like foliage that develops bright red tips under strong light and CO2. Native to the southeastern United States and northeastern Mexico, it is a classic background/midground plant for nature-style aquascapes.
careNotes: Didiplis diandra is the sole species in its genus (Lythraceae, order Myrtales) and is one of the few US-native stem plants in mainstream hobby trade. It produces thin, needle-like leaves on slender stems and is best planted in groups for a bushy effect, with regular trimming to encourage lateral spread. Sources agree on a pH range of about 6.0–7.5; temperature is reported less consistently, but the species occurs naturally in lakes and streams across the southeastern US and northeastern Mexico, so a moderate range of roughly 18–26 °C is appropriate. Submersed height is typically around 10–30 cm (Buce Plant cites 4–12"). Buce Plant rates the plant as 'easy' with CO2 'not necessary,' but realistically the bright green-to-red coloration the species is sold for requires medium-to-high light, CO2 injection, and a nutrient-rich substrate with iron-inclusive fertilization — without these the plant survives but stays plain green and tends to grow leggy. Slow grower compared with many stem plants, which is helpful for layout stability but means it does not aggressively out-compete algae; keep nutrient levels balanced and prune any algae-affected lower stems promptly. Care level is averaged to 'intermediate' here because reliable red tips and dense growth require CO2 and high light, even though the plant itself is forgiving.
breedingNotes: Propagated vegetatively: cut anywhere along a healthy stem and replant the cutting in the substrate. Frequent topping also encourages lateral side shoots from the mother plant, producing the bushy hedge-like growth the common name refers to. Emersed flowering is possible but seed propagation is uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for lighting (moderate-high), CO2 (not necessary but enhances red tips), growth rate (slow), pH (6.0-7.5), height (4-12" ≈ 10-30 cm), care level (easy, averaged up to intermediate), propagation (cut anywhere along stem), and native range (USA lakes and streams). Also confirms family Lythraceae and the 'Peplis Diandra' synonym.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Didiplis
additional.notes: Confirms monotypic genus in family Lythraceae, order Myrtales. Native range 'Southeastern USA to Northeastern Mexico,' extending the Buce Plant 'USA' range to include Mexico. Binomial authority: (Nutt. ex DC.) Alph.Wood.
